data.title: Time To Hunt Bargains In The BDC Forest: 2 Picks At ~20% Discount To NAV
data.description:
My closest followers have most probably noticed that while I am long and bullish on certain BDCs, when it comes to the broader sector perspectives, then my sentiment is quite bearish, to say the least.

So far, I have been focused on BDC dividends and their sustainability levels. The current market dynamics in combination with the state of "limited margin of safety" at which most BDCs are operating at, in my view, creates an excellent set-up for seeing system-wide dividend cuts.

I will not repeat myself, but, in a nutshell, things boil down to this:

  • Sector average base dividend coverage of 102% (on Q2 data).
  • Recent interest rate cuts that will negatively impact Q3 and Q4 2025 financials.
  • Potentially (actually, very likely) new interest rate cuts on the table soon.
  • No clear data that would prove that the sector-wide spread compression has stopped.
  • Sector average debt-to-equity of 1.19x, which means that many BDCs do not have incremental leverage capacity to offset any of these headwinds.

This is bad, and I have not changed my views here at all.

Having said that, it is not that BDCs (BIZD) have lived in a vacuum. The market has recognized these issues and priced the sector accordingly. For example, the sector median P/NAV is 0.88x. So, most BDCs trade at double digit discounts.

And let's keep in mind that these are averages.

There are certain securities that are still relatively richly priced such as Main Street Capital (MAIN) and Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) - trading at P/NAV of 2.01x and 1.37x, respectively. As much as I like MAIN and TSLX, it is hard to justify paying such a high price, while most of the sector is in a discount territory and, importantly, the headwinds that I outlined above are indeed quite negative.

Similarly, there are some deep-value BDCs that trade at even steeper discounts than ~12%. Granted, most of them are very speculative and deserve to be priced so. Think of FSK KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) and TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC Corp. (TPVG) - all with consistent problems on the non-accrual end.

In this heavily discounted area, it could actually be worth spending a bit of time to potentially scoop up bargains. I know that even if we succeed in finding some unfairly punished cases, the chances of them being truly sustainable income producers remain extremely limited. Yet, it does not mean that on a total return basis, the investments would be unattractive. The steeper the discount, the bigger price return potential (through price-to-value convergence).

Let me present you two heavily discounted BDC picks, which even though are not my top BDC income choices (due to lack of significant margin of safety on the dividend coverage end), they certainly carry very attractive total return prospects.

#1: Carlyle Secured Lending- 18% below NAV

Carlyle Secured Lending Inc. (CGBD) is a typical BDC, which focuses on first-lien loans in the $25+ million EBITDA company segment. It is not a venture capital biased one nor a vehicle that goes heavy on equity co-investments such as Main Street Capital and Fidus Investment (FDUS). There is also no exposure to CLOs or aggressive loan investment structures.

Namely, we can make a safe argument that CGBD is an average/standard BDC with no specific nuances that might justify abnormal valuations.

So, the question is then why CGBD trades at almost 10% discount to already discounted BDC peer base. Frankly, I do not see a reason for that. In fact, I would even argue that CGBD should deserve slight premium over the sector median, which has a P/NAV of 0.88x. Now, I am not saying that CGBD has the necessary properties for being priced at NAV (not even speaking about premium), but something closer to, say, Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) could be justified.

I will give you three main reasons.

First, CGBD has already managed to stabilize its NII per share generation by registering only $0.01 per share decline relative to the Q1 2025 period. Here it is important to mention that the portfolio weighted average debt yield level has remained unchanged in Q2 at 10.9%, which is largely in line with the prevailing sector average levels. In other words, CGBD does not have a yield level from which there could be a meaningful fall.

Second, speaking of NII per share stability, it is clear that CGBD is exposed to some major headwinds (most importantly, the lowering of interesting rates). Even though I doubt that CGBD will manage to sustain the current NII per share levels, it has at least a slight (relative) advantage of having a spare debt capacity on its books that could be used to fund portfolio growth. As of Q2 2025, CGBD's debt to equity stood at 1.1x, while the sector average is closer to 1.2x.

Third, and this is also something we can consider a relative advantage, CGBD has all of its external leverage loans tied to SOFR. What this means is that compared to most other peers, which carry some exposure to fixed rate debt, CGBD should be able to maximize the benefit of reduced financing costs (albeit, on a net basis, the effects are negative).

Now, we could theoretically make a case that CGBD deserves a higher discount than the sector median because of the non-accrual rate, which ending Q2 was at 2.1% (of FV). Yet, the thing is that right after Q2 2025, CGBD managed to restructure the struggling loan case, thereby bringing down the non-accrual rate to only 1.0%, which is definitely below sector average.

Another issue, which is the main reason for me to not become super bullish on CGBD, is the base dividend coverage of 98%. It means that the current dividend is underfunded; hence the dividend cut risk is high.

Yet, let me put this in the following context:

  • Currently, CGBD's yield is 12.6%. Assuming a ~25% dividend cut, the yield would still remain quite enticing and actually in line with the sector average if we carve out such extremes as Prospect Capital (PSEC), Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and Oxford Square Capital Corp. (OXSQ) - these yield close to 20%.
  • Most of the key fundamental metrics, as described above, are at above average territory in terms of their quality and the embedded margin of safety.

So, I think that it is rather obvious that CGBD has been unfairly punished, at least compared to the rest of the sector. For patient and value-oriented investors, CGBD could be an interesting choice to consider for potential alpha (juicy income and notable price gain potential).

#2: Barings BDC, 17% below NAV

Barings BDC Inc (BBDC) has a very similar story to that of CGBD.

At its core, BBDC is a traditional BDC with a simple business model that is centered around mostly first-lien lending to well cash-flowing enterprises that generate EBITDA in the range of $25 million to $75 million. There is no material exposure to equity products, CLOs or venture capital investments.

So, CGBD is subject to almost exactly the same headwinds as CGBD and the lion's share of the sector. Yet, somehow we see that when it comes to P/NAV, it trades well below the already discounted sector median.

I will start with couple of things that differentiate BBDC from CGBD (in a positive manner).

Based on Q2 2025 data points, the dividend coverage level was 108%, which is better than for CGBD, better than the sector average of 102%, and good for incremental shock absorption, which will inevitably come from lower interest rates. At least from this perspective, the event of BBDC trimming its dividend lies farther in the future than for CGBD. Importantly, the NII per share generation has clearly stabilized in Q2.

Moreover, BBDC's weighted average portfolio debt yield (10.1%) is rather low, at one of the lowest levels in the sector, which is, obviously, a notable positive in terms of facing lighter consequences from spread compression. In addition, we have to appreciate BBDC's solid track-record on the non-accruals. Historically, it has managed to keep them well below sector average, at around 1.0% of FV (this includes a period like the COVID-19 pandemic). Currently, the non-accrual rate sits at 0.5% - something that most peers cannot match.

Now, the only negative driver that I could find in BBDC's books is the relatively indebted balance sheet, which as of Q2 2025 had a debt to equity of 1.33x. It is more than clear that from this kind of level it is almost impossible to secure incremental portfolio growth without becoming overly aggressive. My expectation is that BBDC slowly but surely works off the leverage to a more balanced level of around 1.15x to 1.25x, which is actually what has been the BDC's long-term average. This process is inherently negative for the NII per share generation, especially in the context of forthcoming pressures.

Having said that, we have to always put all of the variables together and assess them collectively to see whether the entire equation leads indeed to a justified valuation (in this case, a massive discount).

In my humble opinion, there is no substantiated reason behind why BBDC should trade at this kind of discount to the sector median.

So, I would say that BBDC is one of the few BDC bargains out there that offers high dividends in combination with strong upside potential.

data.title: Virtus Westchester Event-Driven Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

In the first half of 2025, the landscape for mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and corporate reorganizations showed significant momentum. Dealmakers have been active, despite the complex macro environment, which included elevated interest rates, tariff concerns, and regulatory uncertainties. Global M&A activity surged 30% versus this time last year. Year-to-date transaction volume reached $2.2 trillion as of June 30, signaling renewed corporate confidence and expectations for clarity around regulations.

The final two months of the second quarter were particularly strong, despite challenges created by President Trump’s proposed new tariffs. However, resilient markets and easing antitrust enforcement spurred activity, with multiple large deals expected in the latter half of 2025.

The U.S.’s $1.2 trillion total dominated global M&A activity. Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) tallied $659 billion, up 12% year-over-year, and the Asia-Pacific region (APAC), particularly Japan, recorded its second-highest deal volume ever. This global surge was supported by a more deal-friendly regulatory environment and easing trade tensions.

Corporate reorganizations, such as spinoffs and split-offs, generated $237 billion in volume this year. Sectors such as energy and technology (focused on AI and cybersecurity) are leading this trend. We look for transactions with strong fundamentals and strategic alignment to maximize the odds of transaction success. Financial sponsors, such as private equity firms, drove $456 billion in transactions year-to-date, for 38% of the total M&A volume.

Performance

The Virtus Westchester Event-Driven Fund I-shares (MUTF:WCEIX)(Fund) returned 2.31% in the second quarter, bringing year-to-date performance to 4.43%. Reflecting an almost 4:1 ratio of winners to losers, 59 positions posted positive gains, while 16 had negative marks-to-market. There were no broken transactions. In addition, 15 deals were completed, with capital redeployed into 17 new situations during the quarter. The subcategories of arbitrage and opportunistic credit recorded positive gains, contributing 1.95% and 0.69%, respectively. Special situations and restructurings experienced mark-to-market losses of -0.22% and -0.11%, respectively, in the quarter. As of the end of June, we held 48 positions and were approximately 125% invested.

Positive Contributors

United States Steel Corp./Nippon Steel Corp. (OTCPK:NPSCY)(63 basis points [bps]): The $14.9 billion acquisition of U.S. Steel by Nippon Steel, finalized on June 18, 2025, overcame significant regulatory and political hurdles. Initially blocked by President Biden, the Trump administration secured the U.S. government a “golden share,” commitments to $11 billion in investments, and 100,000 jobs, which paved the way for its eventual approval. As one of the few arbitrage funds that remained committed to this opportunity, our patience and strategic conviction paid off, delivering strong value as the deal closed.

Discover Financial Services/Capital One Financial Corp. (COF)(32 bps): The $35.3 billion all-stock acquisition of Discover Financial Services by Capital One Financial Corporation, finalized on May 18, 2025, was our second-largest contributor to portfolio performance in 2Q. The deal created the largest U.S. credit card issuer by loan volume, and the sixth-largest bank by assets. Our position capitalized on the deal’s attractive spread, delivering strong risk-adjusted returns as the transaction closed.

Mauser Packaging Solutions (31 bps): We originally purchased the company’s 7.25% 2025 bonds several years ago, anticipating a sale to another private equity firm. That deal fell through, and in 2023, Mauser exchanged these bonds for 9.25% 2027 bonds with a higher coupon and longer maturity, plus a fee. Spreads stabilized throughout the quarter and the bonds recovered toward their historical trading range.

Performance Detractors

Becton, Dickinson & Company (BDX)(-38 bps): In April 2025, the company announced its plan to spin off its Biosciences and Diagnostic Solutions business, targeting completion in fiscal 2026. In 2Q, the position incurred a mark-to-market loss driven by mixed financial results in the Biosciences unit, which reported weaker-than-expected margins, broader market volatility, and analyst downgrades citing reduced research funding in China and the U.S., a U.S. government ban on certain exports to China, and tariff uncertainties.

Macro Portfolio Hedge (-27 bps): Our macro portfolio hedge, designed to mitigate downside risk, provided a buffer during the April market sell-off following President Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcements. However, the subsequent market rallies in May and June reversed these gains, turning the hedge into a detractor.

Kellanova/Mars Inc. (K)(-25 bps): The $35.9 billion acquisition, announced in August 2024, remains a high-conviction investment, despite ongoing regulatory hurdles. The U.S. Federal Trade Commission cleared the deal on June 25, 2025, without conditions, securing 27 of 28 required regulatory approvals. However, the European Commission’s Phase II antitrust investigation delayed the expected closing to late 2025. Despite this, the potential for strong risk-adjusted returns, even with possible divestitures under $750 million, supports our commitment to this transformative transaction.

Conclusion

The remainder of 2025 appears poised for increasing activity, supported by resilient corporate confidence and easing U.S. regulatory conditions, though some challenges remain. Ongoing European regulatory scrutiny, potential tariff-related disruptions, and geopolitical uncertainties could affect the pace of announcements and widen spreads for some transactions—offering opportunities to deploy capital at potentially more attractive levels. Strategic consolidations and spinoffs are likely to remain active, with financial sponsors continuing to deploy capital. Our portfolios are positioned to navigate these dynamics, focusing on transactions with the perceived highest risk-reward profile, and filtering for high-probability opportunities.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet ABS/MBS ETF (VABS) Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Securitized markets were volatile in April as tariff announcements drove spreads 10–15 basis points wider month-over-month. Early-April volatility temporarily stalled new issuance, but supply rebounded sharply once conditions stabilized. In May, stronger-than-expected employment data, widening fiscal deficits, and a partial tariff rollback led to a backup in interest rates. However, spreads on private-label securitized products tightened meaningfully versus Treasuries. After an issuance lull tied to the “Liberation Day” tariff shock, May became the most active new-issue month of the year. Investors responded with increased demand—particularly for subordinate tranches—as they sought to add yield and leverage to portfolios. June brought spread tightening, fueled by robust demand and a surge in primary issuance. With Treasury yields falling, securitized products delivered positive total returns to close out the quarter.

Sector Performance

The Fund has significant exposure to asset-backed securities (ABS)—its allocation to ABS returned 1.68% for the quarter. The U.S. consumer credit fundamentals remained generally healthy in 2Q, though signs of inflation stress emerged. Roughly 11% of consumers were making only minimum credit card payments—the highest in 12 years, per the Philadelphia Federal Reserve. Delinquencies also edged higher among both prime and subprime borrowers, though ratings agencies continued to upgrade consumer ABS deals thanks to steady deleveraging. That said, jobs data remained solid with 4.2% unemployment, while used car prices held firm, supporting recovery rates on auto loan defaults. While full-year ABS issuance is steady, it trails 2024 levels. Relative to short corporates, ABS looks historically cheap.

The Fund’s other large exposure is non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS)—its allocation to RMBS returned 1.57% for the quarter. By the end of April, RMBS spreads retraced about a third of their tariff-driven widening, and valuations firmed. The recovery in risk/reward brought investors back, and new issue volumes picked up accordingly. Mid-quarter, spreads continued tightening, returning to 2025 averages. Investor interest remained strong, particularly in senior tranches, as spreads continued to offer a compelling premium. June brought stability to RMBS credit conditions. Though delinquencies and bankruptcies rose modestly among lower-rated borrowers over the quarter, their levels remained low historically. National housing dynamics were still favorable, but oversupply signs emerged regionally, especially in Florida and Texas. Overall, investor demand supported firm spreads.

Fund Performance

The Fund returned 1.69% at NAV for the quarter versus the ICE BofA 1-3 Year A-BBB US Corporate Index returns of 1.48%.

Positive Contributors

  • Aircraft lease and subordinate auto loan securitizations were positive contributors for the period.
  • The Fund’s exposure to commercial mortgage-backed securities and RMBS produced strong total returns for the quarter.

Performance Detractors

  • Our position in unsecured consumer loans lagged due to shorter durations.
  • Our position in timeshare loans lagged for the quarter as the asset tends to underperform when the yield curve steepens.

These securities are also subject to risks associated with the non-repayment of underlying collateral, including losses to the portfolio. Credit & Interest: Debt instruments are subject to various risks, including credit and interest rate risk. The issuer of a debt security may fail to make interest and/or principal payments. Values of debt instruments may rise or fall in response to changes in interest rates, and this risk may be enhanced with longer-term maturities. Market Volatility: The value of the securities in the portfolio may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies and/or general economic conditions. Local, regional, or global events such as war or military conflict, terrorism, pandemic, or recession could impact the portfolio, including hampering the ability of the portfolio’s manager(s) to invest its assets as intended. Market Price/NAV: At the time of purchase and/or sale, an investor’s shares may have a market price that is above or below the fund’s NAV, which may increase the investor’s risk of loss. data.title: Automatic Data Processing: Back In The Buy Column Now (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A white paper that I saw recently piqued my interest. This came from Berkshire Asset Management (not affiliated with the trillion-dollar juggernaut).

It offered several dividend investing analogies. I have heard of some, including the "Snowball Effect." That compares compounding dividend growth to rolling a snowball down a hill. The longer it rolls, the bigger it gets and the more snow it picks up with each rotation.

The "Income While You Sleep" analogy was also familiar to me. Taking the annual portfolio income and dividing it by 365 days in the year gives one an idea of how much daily passive income a portfolio is producing.

The "Commercial Apartment Building" analogy was the one that was unfamiliar to me. This states that the owner's "rent" is based on a rising share of "tenant" earnings or dividends. So long as the rent is being collected and growing every year, it makes sense not to concern oneself with the value of the underlying building constantly (other than to possibly add on buying opportunities).

This brings me to my focus of today, which is Automatic Data Processing (NASDAQ:ADP). When I last covered it with a hold rating in January, I thought its next-gen HCM solution, Lyric, was favorable. I also liked the company's financial position. However, shares were modestly overvalued.

Eight months later, the fundamentals remain respectable. ADP beat expectations for Q4 2025 and issued decent guidance for FY 2026. ADP's balance sheet remains AA-rated by S&P. The improved valuation has enhanced the total return outlook enough that I'm upgrading shares back to a buy rating for the first time since last August.

ADP Closed Out FY 2025 With Strength

On July 30th, ADP released its financial results for the fiscal fourth quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's revenue grew by 7.5% year-over-year to $5.13 billion during the quarter. This exceeded Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter by $50 million.

For more color, that was the 19th quarter out of the past 20 that ADP generated more revenue than the analyst consensus (the exception being Q1 2024).

What was behind this laudable topline growth for the fiscal fourth quarter? In absolute terms, ADP's revenue growth during the quarter was mostly driven by a 7.3% uptick in revenues, other than interest on funds held for clients and PEO revenues to $3.16 billion in the quarter. As was the case in January, the Workforce Software acquisition, which closed last October, was one growth catalyst. Continued U.S. job growth (albeit at a slower pace) in the period was another tailwind.

PEO revenues rose by 7.2% over the year-ago period to $1.66 billion for the fiscal fourth quarter. In his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call, CFO Peter Hadley pointed to a 3% average worksite employee growth rate to 761,000 as the catalyst for this growth. That was fueled by strong new business bookings and stable pay-per-control growth.

Finally, ADP's interest on funds held for clients surged by 11.2% year-over-year to $307.8 million during the fiscal fourth quarter. This was due to a combination of a 6% increase in average client funds balances to $38.1 billion and a 20 basis point increase in the average interest yield on client funds to 3.2%.

ADP's adjusted diluted EPS climbed 8.1% higher over the year-ago period to $2.26 in the fiscal fourth quarter. That edged out the analyst consensus by $0.03 for the period. This was the 20th quarter in as many quarters that ADP achieved this feat.

The company's non-GAAP profit margin was relatively unchanged at 18% during the fiscal fourth quarter. ADP's diluted average share count was 0.7% lower, which is how its adjusted diluted EPS growth outpaced revenue growth in the quarter.

Moving forward, the outlook for ADP remains optimistic. This isn't just blind optimism, either. There are plenty of reasons to be upbeat about the future of the company.

Innovation is a big reason. In FY 2025, the company launched its next-gen HCM solution, ADP Lyric. The company increased the number of clients sold by more than 50% over this time. New logos represented the majority of these new Lyric sales. Live clients doubled versus the prior year, and the pipeline continues to grow.

ADP continues to take action to bolster its AI-driven capabilities. Since its launch in FY 2024, the company has continued its rollout of ADP Assist. In FY 2025, ADP Assist saw millions of interactions with its clients in FY 2025.

The company is also boosting its global payroll opportunities. The company is expanding its offerings in markets with growth opportunities like Japan and Saudi Arabia.

On the acquisition side, ADP acquired the payroll business in late January. The acquisition of this Mexico-based payroll solutions provider added to the company's geographic reach.

That's why ADP is guiding for 5% to 6% revenue growth for FY 2026. Adding in incremental margin expansion and share repurchases, the HCM giant expects 8% to 10% adjusted diluted EPS growth this fiscal year.

Overall, I believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is realistic, if not a bit conservative (given ADP's ability to outperform expectations). For FY 2026, adjusted diluted EPS is expected to rise by 9.1% to $10.92. In FY 2027, an additional 9.6% increase in adjusted diluted EPS to $11.97 is the current consensus. For FY 2028, ADP's adjusted diluted EPS is anticipated to jump by 10.3% to $13.20.

Shifting to the balance sheet, ADP's financial picture remains outstanding. As of June 30th, 2025, the company had over $7.8 billion in cash and cash equivalents/short-term marketable securities on its balance sheet. Against the $8.7 billion in commercial paper borrowings and long-term debt, this is what backs up the AA- S&P credit rating. That provides the company with flexibility to make incremental acquisitions in the future, while also maintaining ample liquidity (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead came from ADP's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and ADP's Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Is Approaching $320 A Share

Since my prior article, ADP's shares have posted a -2% total return. That lagged the 10% total return of the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

For my money, I believe this has swung shares from a hold to a buy rating. ADP's stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 26.1.

For more context, that's below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 28.6 (up slightly from the previous 10-year average multiple of 28.3), per FAST Graphs. The dip in shares and a higher 12-month forward adjusted diluted EPS input have brought the forward 12-month P/E ratio down from 29.1 in January.

Moving forward, I think the ADP's shares remain worth the 10-year average P/E ratio of 28.6. That's because the company's annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus of 9.7% is comparable to the 10-year average annual growth rate of approximately 13%.

This is especially the case considering that ADP routinely beats analysts' adjusted diluted EPS forecasts. In other words, I believe that the company's low-double-digit annual growth prospects remain intact.

As was also the case in January, ADP's non-GAAP profit margin continues to expand. Over the last five years, this metric has improved from 17.6% in FY 2020 to 19.9% in FY 2025.

As of now, ADP's FY 2026 is 23% behind us. That leaves another 77% of FY 2026 and 23% of FY 2027 yet to come over the next 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $11.16.

Using my fair value multiple of 28.6, I compute a fair value of $319 a share. Compared to the $292 share price (as of September 22nd, 2025), that equates to a 9% discount to fair value.

If ADP lives up to growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could generate a 12% annual total return by the end of September 2026. Through FY 2031, the stock could deliver a 12% annual total return.

A Big Dividend Boost Is Around The Corner

ADP's 2.1% forward dividend yield registers meaningfully higher than the industrials sector median of 1.5% (the spread is up 10 basis points from January). That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System awards B and B- grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (the latter being up from a C+ grade in January).

ADP's solid starting income is made even better by the fact that it has been a remarkably consistent dividend grower. For the last 50 years, the company has hiked its payout to shareholders. This is much better than the sector median of just 4 years. That's what earns it an A+ grade for overall dividend consistency from the Quant System.

As if that wasn't enough, it looks like ADP is set up to continue to extend this dividend growth streak. That's because the company's payout ratio is likely to be in the high-50% range for FY 2026 (assuming a 10.4% hike in the quarterly dividend per share to $1.70 in November).

This is just below the 60% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry, per the Zen Research Terminal. What's more, the free cash flow payout ratio is poised to be in the low-50% range in FY 2026. These values explain why the Zen Research Terminal forecasts a 1% probability of a dividend cut in the next 12 months and a 3% risk in the next average recession. The Quant System's A+ grade for overall dividend safety jives with this data, too.

What level of dividend growth is realistic in the years ahead? Well, the Quant System anticipates 7.7% annual forward dividend per share growth. This would be above the sector median of 6.8%. In my view, this is arguably two percentage points too low an expectation. That's due to the company's growth prospects and payout ratios that can allow the dividend to grow in line with adjusted diluted EPS. This warrants an A grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth.

Risks To Consider

ADP is a fundamentally encouraging company. However, it has risks that could still hurt or even break the investment thesis.

One risk to ADP is a labor market slowdown. Recent data suggests that the jobs market is weakening. The three-month average of jobs added has fallen from over 200K to start 2025 to just 29K as of August. If this trend continues, the company's financial results could be temporarily impacted to a degree.

As I noted in January, another risk to ADP is the potential for an incident to occur that damages its reputation among clients. This could include a major cyber breach. That could compromise sensitive client data, resulting in lawsuits and regulatory fines. This could trigger an exodus of existing clients and make it more difficult for ADP to bring in new clients.

One final risk to the company is that it faces plenty of competition. An inability to adapt to shifting client preferences could cost it market share. That would weigh on ADP's growth prospects.

Summary: A Core Holding Back On My Buy List

ADP remains a fundamentally promising investment. The company continues to roll out and adapt its product offerings to fit the needs of its clients. This is why I believe that it can keep growing at a healthy rate for the foreseeable future. ADP's balance sheet remains enviable, possessing an AA- S&P credit rating. The dividend is well-covered by both adjusted diluted EPS and free cash flow, positioning it for another generous raise in November.

Shares are also favorably valued, now trading at the 26x forward P/E ratio range that I desired in my last article. This is why I'm upgrading ADP back to a buy rating, and it is my 18th-largest investment holding, making up 1.6% of my portfolio.

data.title: HFRO: Discounted Valuation As A Result Of Underwhelming Performance
data.description:
Overview

Highland Opportunities and Income Fund (NYSE:HFRO) is structured as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its blended portfolio of real estate securities and fixed-income assets. Real estate has been one of the sectors that have lagged behind the performance of the market, which can be attributed to the higher interest rates. Although I believe that the real estate sector is likely to recover now that interest rate cuts are set in motion, HFRO's internal portfolio needs some fundamental changes in order to increase its appeal as a long-term holding.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HFRO's share price has increased by about 5.7%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 15.8% over the same period. HFRO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 6.9% and monthly payouts. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the distributions going forward, as the latest semi-annual report indicates that the fund is struggling to generate a consistent level of earnings.

The fund now trades at a very large discount to NAV valuation, which may be enticing for investors looking for a good deal. However, I believe that this large discount pricing is an indication of the fund's flaws, rather than an attractive opportunity to accumulate shares. I believe there are alternative funds that offer a better value proposition for investors at this time, so it is difficult to recommend a position in HFRO. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that HFRO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, HFRO has total managed assets of $874M that is spread across 61 different holdings. The fund specifically focuses on real estate securities and aims to invest at least 25% of its assets into other assets that are indirectly secured by real estate. The idea here is that this blended approach will align the fund to capture both capital appreciation and a high level of income generation over different market cycles. So the breakdown of the fund by asset is as follows:

  • Real Estate: 67.5%
  • Equity: 20%
  • Loans: 11.8%
  • Collateralized Loan Obligations: 0.8%

Looking at the breakdown by sector, we can see that HFRO is most heavily allocated towards Real Estate at 92.3%. This is followed by exposure to Health Care and different LLC Interests, which accounts for 16.3% and 9.6% of assets respectively. However, Real Estate makes up so much of the portfolio that investors should anticipate this focus area to have the greatest impact on HFRO's daily price movement and total return potential.

When looking at the top ten holdings, we can see that HFRO is most heavily allocated to private investments. The only publicly listed holding I can see on the list below is NexPoint Real Estate Finance (NREF). Meanwhile, all of the other top holdings seem to be private investments, which may have a higher risk/reward profile for investors. Additionally, we can see that HFRO chooses to structure a meaningful portion of their investments as debt securities. Therefore, I believe that investors should limit their expectation on the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation over time.

In order to demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance chart of HFRO since its public inception. As we can see, HFRO's share price has declined by over 57.1%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of about 17.5%. Therefore, I believe that investors should expect the majority of their returns to come from the fund's ability to continue paying out high distributions over time.

Valuation & Risk

Since HFRO is structured as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, HFRO now trades at a large discount to NAV of approximately 42.21%. For reference, HFRO has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 35.89% over the last five-year period. However, this large discount valuation doesn't necessarily indicate that this is a good time to accumulate shares and it could be a sign of the internal weakness of the fund.

To get better clarity into the fund's internal performance, I reviewed the latest semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.22 per share, while HFRO experienced net realized losses of about $0.18 per share. Looking back on prior years, HFRO's net investment income levels have been wildly inconsistent, which makes it difficult to predict how the fund will perform going forward. For instance, HFRO experienced strong net investment income of $1.35 per share in 2022, but then saw its net investment income decline to $0.53 per share two years later in 2024.

Additionally, the fund has struggled to generate positive net realized gains over the last few years, which I attribute to the higher interest rate environment. The problem is that the fund's inconsistent net investment income means that HFRO is reliant on positive market momentum so that it can capture net realized gains. The only problem is that HFRO is unable to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased, if the real estate sector is trading sideways or down. This reliance on net realized gains has limited the fund's overall ability to provide meaningful NAV growth over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $13.32 per share
  • 2021: $14.29 per share
  • 2022: $13.89 per share
  • 2023: $12.74 per share
  • 2024: $12.00 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $11.38 per share

As part of the fund's operating strategy, HFRO utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings when attractive opportunities are available. The current use of leverage sits around 28.01% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $245M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this higher interest rate environment and it can increase the fund's overall risk profile. Investors need to also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated.

The higher interest rate environment means that it is more costly for HFRO to utilize debt. The spread between the cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities can become a lot thinner, which makes it more difficult to utilize leverage efficiently. The inclusion of leverage and focus on real estate has caused HFRO to demonstrate a strong vulnerability to shifts in the interest rate environment.

Looking at the chart below, we can see how HFRO's share price trended downwards for over two years straight, following the interest rate increased throughout 2022. Now that the Fed has implemented a 25 basis point cut, HFRO's share price has seen a bit of a shift in momentum since optimism around the sector has increased. So while interest rate cuts can serve as a momentary growth catalyst, I believe that the fund's underlying strategy needs to be refined in order for the fund to have any long-term value.

For instance, investors that wanted both exposure to the real estate sector and a high income potential, would be much better off with peer funds. One of the most notable choices out there would be Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI). Just to demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance chart to compare the two funds. As we can see below, RQI significantly outpaces HFRO in total return, which may be attributed to its more straightforward portfolio approach.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0385 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.9%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.462 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, HFRO has demonstrated that it has trouble generating a sufficient level of earnings to actually support payouts. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.33 per share
  • 2021: $1.82 per share
  • 2022: $0.44 per share
  • 2023:($0.23) per share
  • 2024: ($0.60) per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): ($0.05) per share

Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that HFRO previously cut the distribution at the start of 2024. This cur was needed in order to help slow the rate of NAV deterioration since the underlying earnings were weak. If earnings do not improve over the next twelve months, I believe that HFRO will need to cut the payouts again in order to preserve the overall health of the NAV. For this reason, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in HFRO for income investors that are seeking a reliable source of income. The main reason why investors maintain exposure to these high-yield assets is for the income that it can provide. If that income is threatened, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position.

Additionally, it's worth noting that the fund primarily covers distributions with net investment income. Therefore, the distributions received from HFRO are not tax-efficient and will primarily be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. So based on your current tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in HFRO would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax implications.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I remain cautious about the value proposition that HFRO offers to investors at this time. The fund trades at a very large discount to NAV valuation, which may seem like an attractive opportunity at first. However, the fund's internal structure has led to underwhelming performance and inconsistent income levels. As a result, I also remain cautious about the sustainability of the distributions at this time. If earnings do not improve, I believe that the fund's payouts will need to be reduced further in order to preserve the health of the NAV.

data.title: Good News And Bad News
data.description:
Institutional exposure to equities is at its highest level since November 2007, and American households’ allocation to stocks has surpassed the 2000 tech-bubble highs. Trading volume on U.S. stock exchanges last week reclaimed last April’s record high.

At the same time, as debt prices have soared, the yield reward that investors are receiving from owning corporate bonds is at its lowest since 1998.

The good news is that the majority of risk assets have rebounded from April’s crash and ripped higher year-to-date.

The bad news is that the more they continue, the more brutal the subsequent sell-off is likely to be - that’s the math.

Of course, not everyone is helplessly long risk securities. A few have preserved and increased their fortunes over time by patiently waiting for more durable and rational opportunities to act on. See, Black Swan Manager Sees Huge Rally, Then 1929-Style Crash:

“I’m the crash guy—I remain the crash guy,” says Mark Spitznagel, who earned $1 billion in a single day for his clients during 2015’s “Flash Crash.” A protégé of “Black Swan” author Nassim Nicholas Taleb, his hedge fund, Universa Investments, also scored major gains when Lehman collapsed and when Covid-19 sparked a meltdown.

The alarming part of Spitznagel’s current outlook is that he sees conditions akin to 1929, the year of the Wall Street crash. The silver lining for those hoping the bull-market music will keep playing a while longer: He thinks this is more like the early part of 1929 when stocks added significantly to their Roaring ’20s gains.

In fact, since 1980, the S&P 500 has returned an above-trend 26% annualized in the 12 months preceding the start of each bear market (shown below).

Booming markets tend to attract capital with increasingly wild abandon. The bad news is that subsequent bear markets take back years of capital gains and more.

Disclosure: No positions

data.title: Virtus Seix High Yield Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by a dramatic reversal in sentiment, driven by shifting expectations around trade policy, inflation, and monetary policy. After a turbulent start to the quarter triggered by the April 2 “Liberation Day” announcements, markets stabilized and rallied into quarter-end, buoyed by easing inflation data, resilient corporate fundamentals, and renewed investor appetite for risk.

BB and single-B rated bonds performed largely in line with each other during the second quarter. The ICE BofA BB U.S. High Yield Index (H0A1) increased by 3.46%, while the ICE BofA Single-B U.S. High Yield Index (H0A2) increased by 3.47%. CCCs outperformed in 2Q25, reversing what we saw during the first quarter as the ICE BofA CCC & Lower U.S. High Yield Index (H0A3) increased by 4.28%. However, for the year-to-date period, BBs outperformed single-Bs and CCCs. The H0A1 index increased by 4.96% for the first half of the year, the H0A2 index increased by 4.20%, and the H0A3 index increased by 3.58%. BBBs, as measured by the ICE BofA BBB U.S. Corporate Index (C0A4), increased by 4.29% for the first half of the year.

The option-adjusted spread for the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index (H0A0) began the year at 292 basis points (bps), widened to 461 bps on April 7 shortly after the Liberation Day tariff announcements, and ended the quarter at 296 bps—almost back to where we began the year.

Performance

For the second quarter, the Virtus Seix High Yield Fund’s (Fund’s) I shares increased by 2.76%. This performance was 71 bps fewer than the benchmark, the ICE BofA US High Yield BB-B Constrained Index (HUC4), which increased by 3.47%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included financials, telecommunications, and gaming & leisure. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included metals & mining, cable satellite, and energy.

For the year-to-date period, the Fund’s I shares increased by 3.15%. This performance was 152 bps fewer than the benchmark (HUC4), which increased by 4.67%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included telecommunications, financials, and gaming & leisure. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included cable satellite, metals & mining, and retailers.

Our longer-term objective is to provide a smoother ride with outperformance through the full credit cycle and the strongest relative performance generated during periods of dislocation when the market is offering a plethora of attractively priced securities.

Current Strategy

As we enter the second half of 2025, our strategy remains grounded in disciplined credit analysis and a focus on the higher-quality segments of the high yield market. The macroeconomic backdrop continues to be defined by elevated policy uncertainty, slowing growth tempered by economic resilience, and sticky inflation. These conditions, combined with valuations that are at the tighter end of their historical range, reinforce our up-in-quality bias. This is further supported by the relative strength of BB-rated issuers, which have demonstrated more stable earnings and greater financial flexibility than CCC-rated issuers, which remain under pressure, with compressed margins and more constrained access to the capital markets. As a result, we remain underweight CCCs.

While fundamentals have begun to show signs of modest erosion, we believe the overall credit quality of the high yield market remains strong. The recent surge in refinancing activity, combined with ample liquidity and muted near-term maturity walls, has helped to push out maturities, which can be helpful in lowering default risk. Leverage for high yield issuers rose to 4.08x in 1Q, though this is still below the long-term average of 4.29x. Interest coverage declined modestly to 4.70x but remains well above the historical median, suggesting high yield issuers have above-average levels of financial flexibility. The increase in leverage and decline in interest coverage was due in part to declining profit margins, which have fallen to a four-year low. These trends reflect a market that is fundamentally sound but increasingly vulnerable to macro headwinds, particularly in lower-rated cohorts.

From a rating perspective, the trends have been less favorable this year. The year-to-date upgrade to downgrade ratio is at 0.88x to 1 (suggesting more downgrades than upgrades this year) compared to 1.22x for the same period in 2024. The upgrade to downgrade ratio was 0.54x in June, which was the second-worst month of the year (April being the worst). Looking beneath the surface, the year-to-date upgrade to downgrade ratios for BBs, single-Bs, and CCCs reveal that higher-quality assets show positive ratings momentum while lower-quality ones trend negatively. This pattern has remained consistent for at least the past year. Retail and consumer products experienced the greatest number of downgrades year-to-date.

From a valuation perspective, spreads remain tight by historical standards, but yields continue to offer attractive entry points, particularly in BBs and select single-Bs. Yields began the year just inside long-term median levels at 7.47%, widened to 8.65% (75th percentile), and ended the quarter at 7.05% (32nd percentile). The relatively attractive yields combined with solid fundamentals has supported trading levels for the higher-quality part of the market.

We remain constructive on high yield, but are maintaining a cautious and selective approach, favoring quality, liquidity, and flexibility as we navigate the remainder of the year. We believe this environment favors active management and security selection, with a focus on issuers that offer compelling risk-adjusted income and downside protection.

data.title: Thermo Fisher: A Wide-Moat Leader With Multi-Year Growth Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Thermo Fisher (NYSE:TMO) shares have been on an incredible upward trajectory during the Covid-19 pandemic. Ever since testing kits launched that helped us regaining more and more of our freedoms, the company producing them became a rockstar seemingly overnight. Revenues jumped approximately 75% in just a three year time span, and the entire investment case changed. No longer was Thermo Fisher a steadily compounding healthcare company, but a high-flying growth stock.

But when the need for testing faded, so did the enthusiasm for the stock. Today, Thermo Fisher shares trade roughly 28% below their 2021 highs, staying inside a $500-600 corridor most of the time. Needless to say, the momentum has completely faded.

However, with pandemic-related effects now in the review mirror, the focus has to shift back to the core business. I am arguing that considering what Thermo Fisher has to offer as a company today, its shares are underpriced. I will present you my thesis on why I think so in the following article, so let’s jump right in!

Thesis

Thermo Fisher acts as a mission-critical supplier and service partner for the global life science industry. The company combines a highly recurring consumables and services base with leading positions in fast growing fields like bioprocessing, cell and gene therapy as well as multi-omics research. With the recent acquisitions of Olink and Solventum’s (SOLV) purification and filtration business, Thermo Fisher has further strengthened its moat as one of the very few players capable of offering an end-to-end solution to its customers. The firm supports partners across the whole value chain, starting with early development and clinical research, all the way to the manufacturing of drugs.

Although near-term growth has been pressured by a cyclical slowdown and constrained biotech budgets because of weak funding, the company’s broad portfolio of offerings provides resilience. Once spending normalizes, its constant drive for innovation and product improvements position the firm well to benefit from secular industry tailwinds, like the outsourcing of pharma R&D, the rising complexity of biologics or the push towards precision medicine.

Thermo Fisher is not just a supplier, but the backbone of modern life sciences, making it a compelling investment opportunity for long-term investors.

Business Breakdown

In the following sections, I would like to give you an overview of what exactly Thermo Fisher has to offer within its broad product and services portfolio. Overall revenues split into four main businesses segments, which in the following I will sort by their contributions. Laboratory Products & Biopharma Services (LPBS) is accounting for roughly 52% of total sales, Life Sciences Solutions (LSS) takes up approximately 21%, with Analytical Instruments (AI) and Specialty Diagnostics (SD) contributing 17% and 10% respectively.

In terms of end markets, Thermo Fisher is primarily catering to the pharma and biotech industries (57%), with academia (15%), the diagnostic sector (15%) as well as the industrial space (13%) accounting for smaller portions of the business.

Laboratory Products & Biopharma Services (LPBS)

The segment sits at the center of Thermo Fisher’s strategy of getting deeply entrenched within its customers business processes. First, the firm offers its own portfolio of consumables for laboratories, like bottles, pipettes, containers or disposable filters. On top of that, Thermo Fisher also sells basic equipment like freezers or incubators. Its brands Nalgene, Nunc, Gibco or Applied Biosystems are being viewed as standards in the industry of research and bio production.

From a business perspective, the revenues that Thermo Fisher is generating through these sales are highly recurring. Because the products here are considered consumables, repeat orders on a regular basis are common to ensure smooth operations for research labs. Even though these products are not highly engineered, they still enjoy high switching costs. The reason for that are validations for research processes that are costly and time consuming. If a pipette or filter are validated, they usually remain the preferred choice for labs for a very long time. Thermo Fisher can not only offer these consumables in a very high quality, but the firm also possesses the characteristics of a ‘one-stop-shop’, basically allowing customers to procure everything they need from a single source, creating competitive advantages over peers that are not able to offer the same breadth.

Also within the LPBS segments sits Thermo Fisher’s clinical research service offering. Through its contract research organization (CRO) ‘Pharmaceutical Product Development’ (PPD), which Thermo Fisher acquired in 2021, the company can plan and implement studies for pharma and biotech firms. This includes the design and management of phase I to IV studies, the recruitment and monitoring of patients, key laboratory functions as well as the management of data and central regulatory submissions. CROs are the backbone of pharmaceutical companies, but studies are complex, expensive and often run on a global scale. It is practically impossible for smaller biotech firms to efficiently complete studies on their own. Thermo Fisher’s global network of laboratories and research facilities gives it the advantage of faster patient recruitment and a better access to clinics, combined with its decade-long history of completed trials and a great relationship with regulators.

The real moat starts to surface when customers not only use Thermo’s CRO service, but also take advantage of its Contract Development & Manufacturing (CDMO) capabilities. By acquiring Patheon in 2017, Thermo Fisher evolved into an end-to-end platform for pharma and biotech firms. These companies are increasingly outsourcing development and production because of a lack of capacities or regulatory approvals. Thermo Fisher can step right in by developing small molecules or biologics, helping with drug formulations and doing the clinical and commercial production, including the sterile bottling of drugs, packaging, proper storage and distribution. Patheon essentially accompanies its customers from the pre-clinical phase to the market. The moat here is really strong, as regulatory hurdles for building a CDMO business from scratch are high. Thermo Fisher operates a global network of production facilities that provides customers with flexible capacity.

Another underrated aspect of the segment is the distribution platform that Thermo Fisher provides. It is a global E-commerce network with more than 2.5 million products that offers both, Thermo Fisher’s own laboratory products as well as third-party alternatives. It works as the connecting link between the manufacturer and the end customer, even though Thermo Fisher is obviously prioritizing its own products on the platform. With the vast offering of products, it acts as a standard in electronic and automated procurement processes for labs, once again embodying that one-stop-shop characteristic. It gives labs and research facilities the option for just-in-time deliveries through a large logistics network, however, the advantages for Thermo Fisher should not be underestimated as well. It creates network effects, because with more customers using the platform, the more attractive it gets for competitors to also offer their products there. Because customers are integrating their workflows and ERP-systems with the platform, switching costs are high.

Overall, Thermo Fisher’s LPBS segment creates the option for cross-selling possibilities, like the Accelerator Drug Development solution that the firm is offering to customers, combining all the capabilities it obtained through various acquisitions over the years and decades of expertise in the field. For customers, integrating all these services into a single platform comes with the advantage of faster time-to-market and procurement security, leading to high stickiness of the service.

In the second quarter, LPBS grew +4.1% y/y, with a margin expansion of 90bps to 13.8%. As we can see in the chart below, the market for CDMO services is expected to grow significantly over the coming years, presenting a tailwind for Thermo Fisher as the second biggest player in the industry behind Lonza Group (OTCPK:LZAGY), which is a pure-play in the space.

The structural trend of outsourcing is related to the rising complexity of active ingredients as well as increasing R&D budgets for pharma and biotech companies that are putting pressure on them to save costs, benefitting companies like Thermo Fisher that are capable of producing in an efficient and reliable way. The expected growth of cell and gene therapies will be another boost to CDMO demand, as the market is forecasted to grow at a 38% CAGR through 2028.

Life Sciences Solutions (LSS)

Within LSS, Thermo Fisher’s LPBS segment combines a series of core technologies for modern bioscience. These include bio process solution like cell culture media or reagents, PCR or Next-Generation-Sequencing (NGS) kits for genetic analysis, cell and gene therapy equipment like CRISPR technology as well as kits and assays for protein and molecular analysis.

Thermo Fisher’s advantage here is similar to its LPBS segment, as cell culture media need to be validated over a multi-year horizon, creating high switching barriers. On top of that, customers that are working with PCR, NGS or bio process media are continuously buying reagents and consumables, generating recurring revenues for Thermo Fisher.

One important growth driver for the segment is the rising importance of biologics and biosimilars. The market is forecasted to grow at a 7.6% CAGR through 2035 to a value of over $1.1 trillion, creating high demand for bioprocess media.

Another crucial driver is the shift towards precision medicine. Instead of creating one therapy that fits everybody, modern medicine is trying to find the right therapy for the right patient. For that, bio markers are needed, which are measurable signals inside a patient’s body that are showing how diseases are developing as well as if and how a specific medicine is impacting its progression. Those bio markers could be genes, proteins or metabolic products. This is where so called ‘Omics’ come into play, which include Genomics, Transcriptomics, Proteomics and Metabolomics. For a lot of medical problems, one of these areas is needed, i.e. doing a gene test for hereditary diseases. For complex diseases like cancer, Alzheimers or autoimmune diseases, we need Multi-Omics: a combination of all of these areas. Thermo Fisher is providing all the necessary equipment and chemicals that are needed to generate the data necessary to develop therapies and medicines for these diseases.

The Multi-Omics market is projected to more than triple over the coming 8 years, presenting a huge growth opportunity for Thermo Fisher to capitalize on, being one of only a few, if not the only company that can offer the full Omics stack to customers today.

The LSS segment grew 6.1% organically y/y in the latest quarter, expanding its margins by 10bps to 36.8%. Non-organic growth was coming in at 6% y/y, which results from the recent acquisition of Olink in 2024. The company is a leading provider of proteomics equipment that helps Thermo Fisher strengthen its Omics portfolio. Additionally, the company recently completed its acquisition of Solventum’s (SOLV) purification and filtration business. The move completes Thermo’s portfolio for bio production, catching up with its competitors Merck (OTCPK:MKGAF) and Danaher (DHR), which lead the market for filtration solutions. By offering cell media, reagents, disposable systems and filtration kits, Thermo Fisher once again strives for becoming the one-stop-shop solution.

Analytical Instruments

Within this segment, Thermo Fisher develops and manufactures high-end instruments that are essential for precisely analyzing molecules, materials and tissue. This includes mass spectrometers for drug discovery, chromatography systems for the separation and analysis of substance mixtures in quality controls for pharmaceutical companies and electron microscopes for structural biology and material science. These instruments are not specific to a single industry, but are being used in pharma and biotech, environmental research, the food industry or even semiconductor development, which makes the segment less cyclical.

Thermo Fisher is a market leader for high-end mass spectrometers with its Orbitrap, Astral and Stellar series, as well as being a global leader for electron microscopes. In some of the other areas I mentioned, the firm enjoys a leading position, but to a lesser extent compared with these two markets, as Danaher, Agilent (A), Waters (WAT) and Bruker (BRKR) are also taking prominent market shares.

The AI segment offers a mix of cyclical sales that are dependent on lab budgets and recurring revenues from maintenance and repair services, as well as subscription revenues from associated software solutions that are working in tandem with the instruments for data analysis purposes.

In Q2 of 2025, AI revenues decreased -3% y/y because of lower instrument budgets overall. I expect new product cycles to be a catalyst for future growth as when budgets recover. Even though Thermo Fisher is considered to be a company in the broader healthcare sector, its electron microscopes are essential to the chip and battery industries, which are both growing at really fast rates driven by the boom in artificial intelligence and electric vehicle adoption.

Specialty Diagnostics (SD)

The segment that lifted Thermo Fisher to unprecedented highs during the pandemic because of the incredible demand for Covid tests finally left those special effects behind, so it can once again focus on its core business. That includes diagnostic testing kits and systems for hospitals and labs, like antibody tests for allergies and autoimmune disease, tests that verify infections or toxicological tests. Thermo Fisher is not competing with giants like Abbott (ABT) or Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) in mass markets, but rather fills its own niche that has high requirements for quality and offers higher margins. Regulatory barriers are high and validation efforts are once again leading to competitive barriers.

Even though revenues increased by just 1.5% y/y in Q2 2025, the segment is attractive for Thermo in an overall portfolio context, since diagnostics are usually independent of research and development budgets of laboratories and clinics, as they face stable demand throughout cycles. But that doesn’t mean that there is no underlying growth path. An aging population comes with the need for more diagnostics, as chronic diseases are getting more common, creating solid potential going forward.

Valuation

To determine whether Thermo Fisher is a good investment right now, we have to take a look at the relative valuation versus industry peers.

EV/EBITDA (FWD) EBITDA Margin (TTM)
Thermo Fisher (TMO) 19.15 25.03%
Danaher (DHR) 20.05 31.32%
Agilent Technologies (A) 19.41 27.08%
Waters Corporation (WAT) 16.73 34.65%
Bruker (BRKR) 13.67 16.20%
Lonza Group (OTCPK:LZAGY) 18.67 27.42%
Sartorius (OTCPK:SARTF) 17.76 22.52%
IQVIA (IQV) 12.01 18.62%
Abbott Laboratories (ABT) 20.64 26.57%
Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY) 10.07 36.90%
Average 16.82 26.63%

Using an EV/EBITDA relative valuation method, I calculated the average multiple for the peer group of life science services and equipment companies, which is 16.82 at the time of this article.

This indicates a slight relative overvaluation of Thermo Fisher’s shares at the moment. However, I personally think it is reasonable, since the company is by far the most diversified of the group and has a leading role in all important fields of the life sciences industry, positioning the firm attractively to benefit from underlying growth potential. Considering the company has been trading sideways for a number of years now, I believe that the valuation has come down enough to make it an appealing buying opportunity worth considering.

Risks to the Thesis

Even though I view Thermo Fisher as an attractive investment opportunity, there are some important risks to consider before making a purchase. First, some parts of the business are cyclical in nature, including the Analytical Instruments segment. While this just represents a small part of overall revenues, a continued weakness in research and investment budgets would be a drag on the segments recovery potential. The same could be true for CRO and CDMO services that are usually tied to R&D spending of pharma and biotech companies, making it more susceptible to macroeconomic slowdowns.

Another risk is related to tariffs, since Thermo Fisher has a massive global footprint. If trade policies would get worse from here, margins could be negatively impacted.

Additionally, as Thermo Fisher’s growth strategy is heavily focused on acquisitions, problems that stem from business integrations could lead to added costs and reduce synergy potential.

Last but not least, because of high regulatory hurdles typical for the industry as well as fast technological advancements, changes in requirements and breakthroughs could potentially lead to shifts in market shares.

Conclusion

Thermo Fisher has emerged from the pandemic as more than just a temporary Covid winner. It sits at the center of the global life sciences industry. With a highly recurring revenue base, a growing exposure to structural trends like R&D and production outsourcing and strategic acquisitions of Olink and Solventum’s purification and filtration business, Thermo Fisher continues to strengthen its moat. While cyclical headwinds remain, I believe that long-term growth drivers are far outweighing them.

At today’s valuation, I view the company’s shares as attractively priced for long-term investors seeking exposure to the sector. As a result, I initiate coverage on Thermo Fisher with a Buy rating.

data.title: Daily Journal: From Editorial Legacy To Valuation Challenges On The Market
data.description:
DJCO: Business Analysis and Stock Performance

To analyze Daily Journal Corp. (NASDAQ:DJCO) with the necessary rigor, I first need to define what the company is today. While it is impossible to ignore its legacy as a California-based legal publisher, the reality is that as of the nine months ended in fiscal 2025, nearly 77% of its revenues now come from the judicial software vertical.

At first glance, this shift may explain its behavior in the market; investors struggle to value a GovTech business where more than 80% of total assets sit in a securities portfolio. So while the S&P 500 is up 13% YTD and the Dow Jones Media Index is down only –2%, DJCO has already lost –21% so far this year.

I don’t believe this is sector-driven, since the GovTech industry in which DJCO operates shows clear tailwinds. For example, the global GovTech market was valued at $606B in 2024 and could exceed $1.4T by 2034, implying a CAGR of around 9%. These are, in my view, healthy figures that highlight a long runway for growth.

It is also worth emphasizing that one of the most important markets is the United States, where the federal IT budget for 2025 amounts to $75B, with specific allocations for cloud, cybersecurity, and artificial intelligence applied to public administration, areas directly aligned with Journal Technologies.

That is why I believe using a simple P/E ratio can distort the picture, since reported net income fluctuates with realized and unrealized gains on the portfolio. My preference is to rely on EV/Sales or EV/EBITDA to assess the impact of contracts, subscription margins, and growth, and ultimately to apply a sum-of-the-parts approach, as the securities portfolio carries too much weight to be treated as part of the operating business.

Shift Toward Court Software and GovTech

It is now time to look more closely at how the company has evolved from its historic publishing roots. The main engine today is Journal Technologies, created in 2014 after several mergers, which primarily sells judicial software used by courts to digitize case records, enable e-filing to replace paper, and process online payments for fees, fines, and court costs.

The publishing segment still exists, contributing revenues from subscriptions and advertising, but it has become residual. This confirms that the company’s pivot into GovTech was not a test run but rather the new operating core of DJCO.

In revenue terms, as of June 2025, the software vertical generated $53.8M, accounting for 77% of the total mix. Publishing is now increasingly marginal. While filings do not provide a complete geographic breakdown, most Journal Technologies contracts are in the United States, with additional projects in Canada and Australia.

The challenge for DJCO is to maintain and expand this structural dependency within courts, since migrating millions of records or risking disruptions to e-filing processes is neither easy nor desirable. The strategy going forward appears defensive, focused on retaining existing clients, expanding contracts incrementally through modules such as calendaring or external agency communication, and targeting courts that still rely on legacy systems.

Industry growth over the next decade is projected at 8–10%, which suggests DJCO’s trajectory will be steady rather than disruptive. The National Center for State Courts highlighted in 2025 that many U.S. state courts still operate on outdated systems, leading to delays and errors staff can barely mitigate due to limited resources. Even if AI can automate tasks, privacy risks in such a sensitive field will likely force the use of closed systems, which is why I believe DJCO’s proposition has a sustainable competitive advantage.

Multiples, SOTP, and 12-Month Price Target

To begin valuing DJCO, we start with the following figures: market cap of $618M, share price of $447.93, and 1.38M shares outstanding.

The balance sheet shows $25M in borrowings linked to the margin account and about $1M in mortgage notes collateralized by real estate, so total debt is around $26M. With available cash of $18.7M (excluding $2.2M in restricted cash), DJCO's enterprise value stands at about $625M.

In my view, this number is not fully accurate, because, as previously mentioned, the company also holds a securities portfolio worth $443M. Although this cannot be treated as available cash since it is invested and exposed to market volatility and collateral pledges, it represents assets that could be converted into liquidity relatively quickly. This is the first adjustment I make.

If I annualize operating EBITDA from the last nine months and exclude portfolio gains and losses, the figure comes to around $10M to $11M. This adjustment is necessary because while the operating segments generate positive EBITDA, consolidated results are distorted by the portfolio, and only by removing that effect can we see the real business.

On this basis, applying the $625M EV to $10M of projected EBITDA results in an EV/EBITDA multiple of 62x, which is not a defendable valuation. That is why I prefer to use an SOTP approach, separating the two components. From the $625M EV, subtracting $443M of the portfolio leaves an adjusted EV of about $182M for operations. Comparing this with $10M of EBITDA results in an EV/EBITDA multiple of 18.2x, a much more reasonable figure that changes the picture entirely.

Compared with the sector, Tyler Technologies (TYL), a benchmark in GovTech, trades around 34x forward EV/EBITDA, which I consider a demanding multiple. This leads me to think that the market is not punishing DJCO's operating model but rather the difficulty of valuing the combination of its business and its portfolio. Assuming that the operating business grows at least in line with the sector at 8% to 10%, FY26 EBITDA should reach about $11M. If we keep the portfolio value constant at today’s levels, the SOTP implies around $200M in EV for operations plus about $418M net for the portfolio, for a total of $618M.

Divided among 1.38M shares outstanding, this leads to a 12-month price target in the range of $440 to $450 per share. However, this figure must be read with perspective; if the portfolio suffers a significant drawdown, the downside would be justified. My overall reading is that today’s market price already reflects a fair balance of these parts.

What Can Move the Stock?

The main risk I identify for my thesis is the securities portfolio. For example, a 15% drop would mean a loss of about $65M, or roughly $47 per share. Without taking this into account and making the proper adjustments, no defendable thesis can be built.

In addition, in a macro environment of prolonged high interest rates, combined with possible budget cuts in several U.S. states, the adoption of new contracts or the expansion of Journal Technologies’ services could slow down. Because although the federal budget has been significant, there is no real guarantee it will be maintained or increased.

And this is important because it affects both a bearish and a bullish view. For instance, selling additional service modules to courts where it already has contracts could increase ARR by about 10%, which I estimate would translate into roughly $1M in EBITDA.

But overall, the main risks that could move the share price out of the proposed range will be those that impact the securities portfolio, since the core business is likely to maintain relatively stable growth. It would be far less likely to see contract cancellations or judicial budget cuts of significant scale.

Hold Rating and Forward Outlook

To conclude, my rating on Daily Journal Corp. is currently Hold.

It is difficult to value a business that stands between the operations of a judicial software company trading at reasonable multiples and a securities portfolio that dominates the balance sheet and can distort accounting under different market conditions.

That is why I believe a 12-month price target, based on today’s levels and the portfolio value from the latest quarter, should remain in the range already implied by the market, between $440 and $450 per share.

Now, this can be invalidated very quickly depending on what happens with the securities portfolio, since we could see a multiple expansion to levels closer to TYL without any major change in the structure of the core business.

data.title: Beyond Mega Caps: Exploring The S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index
data.description:
By Algreen Bakasa

Introduction

The representation of S&P 100® companies in the S&P 500® has grown over the past two decades: the blue-chip subset of The 500™ accounted for 70% of the large-cap U.S. equity benchmark’s weight at the end of June 2025, up from 45% at the end of 1998. However, market participants may find it beneficial not to overlook the “other 400” members of the S&P 500, as measured by the S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index.

Size: A USD 15 Trillion Market in Its Own Right

The S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index measures the performance of S&P 500 companies that are not members of the S&P 100. Far from a niche slice of the U.S. equity market, this segment represents more than USD 15 trillion in market capitalization - larger than the equities markets of Japan, the U.K. or Canada, and many times the size of the S&P MidCap 400® or S&P SmallCap 600®. Hence, overlooking the other 400 names in The 500 risks missing a substantial share of the global equity opportunity set.

Sectors: Diversification beyond Mega-Cap Tech

The S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index focuses on diversification beyond the blue-chip exposure of the S&P 100, offering broader sector representation and a more balanced perspective on the drivers of large-cap U.S. equity performance.

Exhibit 3 shows that the S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index had more balanced sector weights compared to the S&P 100 at the end of June 2025. For example, the index had a lower weight in Information Technology and Communication Services and a much greater weight in Industrials, Real Estate and Utilities.

The difference in sector composition was not just a recent phenomenon: Exhibit 4 shows that the S&P 100 and the S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index have had distinct sector weights since the 1990s. While the S&P 100 became increasingly dominated by Information Technology and Communication Services, the S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index consistently showed broader representation across GICS® sectors (see Exhibit 4).

Conclusion

The S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index shines a light on the “other 400” large-cap U.S. companies often overlooked by mega-cap dominance. With a market size rivaling that of many countries’ equity markets, and sector weights that broaden perspectives on the drivers of performance, the S&P 500 Ex-S&P 100 Select Index could serve as a complementary lens on U.S. large-cap equities.
